methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $19M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc

data.narrative:
C12 Quantum Electronics is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Matthieu and Pierre Desjardins that has raised $19.4M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 3 public comps (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private peers including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

The valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to next-stage transitions. Using the $19.4M total raised as a valuation proxy, this method implies a post-money valuation of $39M. The peer-median approach was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables in the quantum computing sector—while 7 same-sector peers exist, none of the narrow filtering criteria (stage, valuation tier) yielded the minimum 3 datapoints required for reliable benchmarking.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $33M to $45M, centered at $39M, though this range is derived from a single applicable method and therefore carries material uncertainty. The primary limitation is the scarcity of investable-stage quantum computing peers with disclosed valuation data. Key uncertainties include the appropriate stage-progression multiplier for this particular subsector and the extent to which broader quantum computing comparables reflect C12's specific technical and commercial positioning. Additional method triangulation would require either broader sector peer data or transaction precedents from the quantum computing space.
